coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of polyzwitterionic carbon dots with superior friction and fatigue control behaviors under water lubrication
coredata.dc:creator: Mou, Zihao
Yan, Ruixin
Peng, Jie
Li, Ying
Huang, Zhangyi
Wang, Zhijun
Zhao, Bin
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: Water lubrication is indispensable for industrial domains, meaning that the tribological behaviors of water are essential for practical applications and should be improved. Herein, a class of zwitterionic polyethyleneimine-grafted carbon dots (CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k) were rationally conceived and synthesized by the facile ring-opening reaction between 1,3-propanesultone (PS) and the PEI0.6-10 k skeletons onto CDs-PEI0.6-10 k. The intrinsic hydration effect of zwitterionic groups endowed the CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k with superior dispersibility and lubricity in water. The tribological results confirmed that the CDs-ZPEI10k with copious hydration and adsorption sites not only maximally reduced the wear rate and friction coefficient for water by 66.1 % and 72.5 % but also performed steadily under harsh conditions, which benefited from the strong hydration lubrication and ball-bearing function of CDs-ZPEI10k. The worn surface analyses evidenced that the CDs-ZPEI10k could be compacted into a uniform tribofilm with a thickness of about 320 nm to reduce friction while restraining the fatigue damage on the rubbing steel surfaces. Undoubtedly, the hydration, adsorption, and deposition synergistically induce the excellent tribological performances of CDs-ZPEI10k. This study proposed a feasible strategy for designing and synthesizing polyzwitterionic CDs, enabling a high-efficiency aqueous lubrication system with robust friction and fatigue control behaviors.

TEM images of (b) CDs-PEI0.6k, (c) CDs-PEI1.8k, (d) CDs-PEI10k, (e) CDs-ZPEI0.6k, (f) CDs-ZPEI1.8k, and (g) CDs-ZPEI10k. The insets in panel b-g are the photographs of various CDs (0.5 wt%)/water dispersions under daylight and UV light (365 nm).

(a) XRD patterns, (b) 1H NMR spectra, (c) TGA curves, (d) FTIR spectra, and (e) XPS full-scan spectra of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k and CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k. High-resolution (f) C1s, (g) O1s, (h) N1s, and (i) S2p spectra of CDs-PEI10k and CDs-ZPEI10k.

(a) COF curves, (b) mean COFs and wear rates obtained by (A) water and (B) PEI10k (1.0 wt%)/water, (C) CDs-PEI10k (1.0 wt%)/water, and (D) CDs-ZPEI10k (1.0 wt%)/water lubricants, respectively. (Friction conditions: 30 min, 2 Hz, and 15 N).

(a) COF curves, (b) mean COFs and wear rates obtained by water lubricants with various c of CDs-ZPEI10k. (a–b) Cross-sectional TEM images of the tribofilm on the wear track achieved by CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant. The inset is the SAED pattern of panel b. (c) HRTEM image of the single nanoparticle in the tribofilm matrix. (d) HAADF image, (e–j) EDS elemental maps, and (k) line-scanning results of the selected region containing steel substrate, tribofilm, and Pt-cap layer. (Friction conditions: 30 min, 2 Hz, and 15 N).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of polyzwitterionic carbon dots with superior friction and fatigue control behaviors under water lubrication

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zwitterionic polyethyleneimine-grafted carbon dots (CDs-ZPEI0.6-10k) with copious hydration and adsorption sites enable a high-efficiency aqueous lubrication system with superior friction and fatigue control behaviors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water lubrication is indispensable for industrial domains, meaning that the tribological behaviors of water are essential for practical applications and should be improved. Herein, a class of zwitterionic polyethyleneimine-grafted carbon dots (CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k) were rationally conceived and synthesized by the facile ring-opening reaction between 1,3-propanesultone (PS) and the PEI0.6-10 k skeletons onto CDs-PEI0.6-10 k. The intrinsic hydration effect of zwitterionic groups endowed the CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k with superior dispersibility and lubricity in water. The tribological results confirmed that the CDs-ZPEI10k with copious hydration and adsorption sites not only maximally reduced the wear rate and friction coefficient for water by 66.1 % and 72.5 % but also performed steadily under harsh conditions, which benefited from the strong hydration lubrication and ball-bearing function of CDs-ZPEI10k. The worn surface analyses evidenced that the CDs-ZPEI10k could be compacted into a uniform tribofilm with a thickness of about 320 nm to reduce friction while restraining the fatigue damage on the rubbing steel surfaces. Undoubtedly, the hydration, adsorption, and deposition synergistically induce the excellent tribological performances of CDs-ZPEI10k. This study proposed a feasible strategy for designing and synthesizing polyzwitterionic CDs, enabling a high-efficiency aqueous lubrication system with robust friction and fatigue control behaviors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Most mechanical friction and fatigue problems are solved with lubricants which rely on forming a thin film between friction pairs to avoid solid collision and withstand the load, thus protecting the machine and saving energy [1,2]. Although lubricants are essential for friction and fatigue control, their drawbacks should not be ignored. For instance, most base oils are difficult to degrade or dispose of harmlessly, and many additives are potentially harmful to ecology. Exploring alternative lubricants and additives hopefully balances the environmental burden with the increasing lubrication demand [3–5]. As a sustainable and renewable lubricant, water displays good carrying, cooling, and recycling properties in numerous industrial occasions, such as cutting, grinding, polishing, and drilling systems [6,7]. However, it is hard for water to provide robust lubrication by hydrodynamic film alone. As a result, mechanical failures caused by friction and fatigue often appear in the water lubrication process [8,9]. It is workable to improve the tribological behaviors of water by blending additives, especially hydrophilic nano-additives [10–14].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of low-cost and eco-friendly carbon nanomaterials with structural designability, display many tribological merits toward water lubrication, such as small size (less than 10 nm) and intrinsic hydrophilicity [15–17]. In recent years, many CDs have emerged as promising additives for water lubrication [18–23]. Ding et al. confirmed that the water dispersibility of CDs could reduce the wear and friction of amorphous carbon (a-C) coatings [18]. Xue et al. proved that the ammonium citrate-derived CDs as a green additive profoundly enhanced the anti-corrosion and friction-reducing behaviors of water [19]. Huang et al. illustrated that the ionic liquid-decorated CDs (CDs-ILs) could improve the tribological properties of aqueous lubricant through the collaboration between CDs core and ILs shell [20]. According to the reports mentioned above, the lubrication mechanism of CDs can be summarized into three points: 1) The hydrophilic sites can bind water molecules through hydrogen bonds to provide CDs with hydration lubrication; 2) The charged units promote the deposition of CDs on the friction interfaces by electrostatic adsorption so that the CDs can act as ball-bearings; 3) The deposited CDs along with friction products form the tribofilm, further protecting the rubbing surfaces. Obviously, the CDs with abundant charged and hydrophilic groups are promising to enhance water lubrication by the synergistic effect of hydration, adsorption, and deposition.

Polyzwitterions, containing zwitterionic units and polymeric skeletons, have been used as a class of hydrophilic polyelectrolytes for water lubrication [24–26]. Firstly, polyzwitterionic materials can achieve outstanding water dispersibility by hydration repulsion and ionic solvation [27]. Secondly, polyzwitterions can be adsorbed and deposited on the rubbing surfaces to consolidate the hydration lubrication [28–30]. Moreover, the category and property of zwitterionic groups can easily be tailored to meet specific tribological purposes. Inspired by these, Zhou et al. reported that the polyzwitterionic porous carbon nanosheets and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) as additives showed outstanding dispersibility and lubricity in water, in which zwitterionic units played a crucial role in the formation of tribofilm while endowing robust hydration lubrication [31,32]. Thus, polyzwitterions with copious hydrophilic and charged sites are highly expected to provide nanomaterials with desired hydration, adsorption, and deposition abilities by wrapping and grafting strategies. Considering the designability of CDs, it might be feasible to incorporate the tribological traits between CDs and polyzwitterions toward water lubrication.

In this work, we rationally conceive and synthesize a class of zwitterionic polyethyleneimine-capped CDs (CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k) by the ring-opening process between 1,3-propanesultone (PS) and the PEI0.6-10 k shells onto CDs-PEI0.6-10 k. The physicochemical features of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k are disclosed by structural, morphological, spectral, and thermal analyses in detail. The tribological results of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k as additives for water lubrication are systematically assessed in the reciprocating ball-on-plate wear tester. The underlying water lubrication mechanism of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k is proposed by connecting the tribological results with the worn surface characteristics.

Branched polyethyleneimine (PEI0.6-10 k, > 99.0 %, RG grade) with average molecular weights of 10, 1.8, and 0.6 k were purchased from Merck Chemical Reagents Co., ltd (China). Absolute ethanol (≥99.5 %, AR grade), anhydrous glucose (>99.5 %, RG grade), and 1,3-propanesultone (PS, > 99.5 %, RG grade) were provided by Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Reagents Co., ltd (China). Deionized water was collected from a milli-Q system. Syringe-driven filter (pore size of 0.22 μm) and cellulose ester dialysis tube (MWCO = 3500 Da) were supplied by Shanghai Titan Technology Co., ltd (China).

The CDs-PEI0.6-10 k were prepared by a solvothermal route, as displayed in Fig. 1 a. Briefly, 1.0 g of PEI0.6-10 k and 2.0 g of glucose were mixed into 50 ml of ethanol to produce a suspension. Then, the mixture was sealed into a reactor and heated in a furnace at 180 °C for 12 h. After filtering the solid residues, the filtrate was condensed by evaporating the solvent. The semisolid product was dissolved and further dialyzed in water for 5 days to remove molecular precursors. The purified CDs-PEI0.6-10 k was acquired by freeze-drying the dialysate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of zwitterionic PEI-grafted CDs (CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k were synthesized via a typical ring-opening reaction of PS. First, 1.0 g of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k was dispersed into 50 ml of ethanol under ultrasound. Then, 6.0 ml of PS was dropwise added to the above dispersion under magnetic stirring. Next, the mixture was placed in an oil bath at 35 °C for 4 h under an inert atmosphere. The resulting precipitate was dialyzed in water for 5 days to discard by-products and impurities. The brown CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k powder was gained by lyophilization.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were gained by a JEOL JEM 2100F instrument to disclose the morphological features of CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected by an X’ pert PRO MPD diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation to disclose the crystal structure of CDs. Proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectra were recorded by a Bruker Advance 400 MHz spectrometer using D2O as the solvent to disclose the chemical structure of CDs. Thermogravimetric analyses (TGA) were performed on a Mettler Toledo TGA/DSC 2 analyzer to disclose the thermal stability of CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were determined on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer using the KBr-pellet method to disclose the chemical components of CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was executed by a PHI-500 versa probe III system with a monochromatic Al Kα radiation to disclose the elemental information of CDs. The contact angle photographs of lubricants on a fresh steel surface were captured by a JC2000D1 contact angle meter to disclose the interfacial adhesion behavior of CDs. The Zeta potentials of lubricants are measured using a JS94J2 zeta-sizer to disclose the interfacial adsorption property of CDs.

The HSR-2M friction and wear testing machine furnished with a ball-on-plate configuration was used to conduct the tribological tests under room temperature and humidity. The reciprocally sliding friction pairs were constructed by pressing an immobile ball upon a moving plate. The material of the ball (diameter of 6 mm, roughness of 67.6 nm) and plate (dimension of Ф 40 × 30 × 2 mm, roughness of 11.6 nm) is bearing steel (GCr15) with rigidity of about 60 HRC, an elastic modulus of 208 GPa, and Poisson’s ratio of 0.3. The friction conditions were controlled at the duration of 30 and 300 min, the stroke of 5 mm, the sliding frequencies of 2, 10, and 20 Hz, and the normal loads of 15, 75, and 150 N. The contact pressure was estimated according to the simplified Hertz equation.

, P, E, and R0 are the maximum pressure, vertical load, the elastic modulus of GCr15 steel, and the radius of the steel ball, respectively. The maximum pressure at 15, 75, and 150 N is estimated as 1.61, 2.76, and 3.48 GPa, respectively. Prior to the test, the steel/steel contact surfaces were moistened with enough lubricant. The COF data were monitored and stored in real-time by a pressure transducer with a loading range of 10–200 N. All the tests were performed three times to acquire convincible data. The profiles, volumes, and micrographs of wear tracks were collected by a 3D optical profilometer (CHOTEST, Super View W1).

The rubbed steel plates were repeatedly wiped with ethanol and then stored in a vacuum-drying oven before being characterized. The morphological and elemental features of the rubbed surfaces were disclosed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7800F) coupled with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The elemental proportion and chemical components on the rubbed surfaces were detected by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, PHI-5000versaprobeIII). The microstructural information for the cross-section of the wear tracks was revealed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM2100F) equipped with EDS. The specimen for TEM analysis was extracted by a focused ion beam instrument (FIB, Helios 600i).

The CDs-PEI0.6-10 k are synthesized using glucose as a carbon source and PEI0.6-10 k as surface modifiers. The TEM images in Fig. 1b ∼ 1g demonstrate that all the CDs are monodispersed spherical nanoparticles. The optical photographs display that all the CDs/water dispersions are pellucid under sunlight and emit intense blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light, reflecting the good hydrophilicity and dispersity of all the CDs in water. As displayed in Fig. S1, no visible aggregates can be observed from the CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k (0.5 wt%)/water dispersions in the initial state and after storing for 90 days, confirming the superior dispersion stability of the CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k in water. The average diameters of CDs-PEI0.6k, CDs-PEI1.8k, and CDs-PEI10k are 2.08, 2.81, and 3.08 nm, respectively, demonstrating the positive correlation between the overall sizes of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k and the molecular weight of PEI. A noticeable growth in particle size can be seen from CDs-PEI0.6-10 k to CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k, disclosing the successful grafting of zwitterionic groups onto PEI0.6-10 k skeletons.

As evidenced by Fig. 2 a, the XRD patterns of all the CDs display the close diffraction peaks (2θ ≈ 21.3°) corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphitic carbon [33], implying the nearly identical crystallinity of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k. The deviation and deformation of the diffraction peaks of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k are practically invisible compared with CDs-PEI0.6-10 k, intuitively reflecting the negligible impact of surface modification on the essential features of CDs core [34].

The 1H NMR spectra in Fig. 2b indicate that the CDs-PEI0.6-10 k present the resonance signals of methylene protons attached to various amide and amino groups with chemical shifts of 2.5 to 4.0 ppm [35,36], confirming the covalent grafting of PEI0.6-10 k shells onto CDs core. New signals at about 1.2, 2.0, and 2.9 ppm in the spectra of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k can be assigned to methylene protons of the zwitterions bonded to the PEI0.6-10 k [37]. Additionally, the peaks of 3.5 to 3.8 ppm can be subdivided into three kinds of methylene protons linked to various cationic groups [38], reflecting the sufficient conversion of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k to CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k.

The TGA curves in Fig. 2c prove that the mass loss of all the CDs can be identified as two stages. The mass losses of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k below 100 °C and above 200 °C can be assigned to the evaporation of moisture and the decomposition of organic segments, respectively [39]. Unlike CDs-PEI0.6-10 k, the organic moieties of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k start to degrade at about 240 °C, reflecting the desired thermal stability of zwitterionic groups. The residual mass fractions for CDs-PEI0.6k, CDs-PEI1.8k, and CDs-PEI10k are calculated as 15.9 %, 9.7 %, and 4.6 %, respectively. However, the terminal mass retentions for CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k are about 20.0 %, indicating that the zwitterionic units are easily carbonized and graphitized at high temperatures under an inert atmosphere.

The FTIR spectra of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k and CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k are provided in Fig. 2d. Conjugation of PEI0.6-10 k shells onto CDs core is evidenced by strong adsorption bands at 3465, 2895, and 1647 cm−1, which can be allocated to the stretching vibrations of NH (amines), –CH2– (aliphatic hydrocarbon), and CO (amides), respectively [40]. In the spectra of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k, two adsorption peaks at 1186 and 1035 cm−1 are associated with the stretching vibrations of SO and SO bonds stemmed from the sulfonate groups [41,42], respectively, implying the covalently anchoring of zwitterions on the PEI0.6-10 k skeletons of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k. In addition, a series of peaks representing aliphatic carbon appears in the fingerprint region, further confirming the grafting of ZPEI0.6-10 k onto CDs core.

The XPS spectra of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k and CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k are displayed in Fig. 2e ∼ 2i. The full-scan spectra in Fig. 2e indicate that the CDs-PEI0.6-10 k contain merely C, N, and O, while the CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k consisted of C, N, O, and S, suggesting that the PEI0.6-10 k were covalently grafted onto CDs core and the zwitterionic units were bonded to CDs-PEI0.6-10 k. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) of CDs-PEI10k can be fitted into three peaks at 286.8, 285.4, and 284.5 eV, which can be allocated to CO, CO/CN, and CC/CC, respectively [43]. For the C1s spectrum of CDs-ZPEI10k, the peak of CO slightly shifts to 287.1 eV, and the intensity of CO/CN increase markedly, which can be associated with the coexist of CN+ and CS bonds, respectively [44]. The O1s (Fig. 2g) spectrum of CDs-ZPEI10k can be fitted into three peaks at 532.6, 531.3, and 530.5 eV, corresponding to the SO, CO/CO, and SO species, respectively [45]. The signals at 401.0, 169.1, 168.2, and 166.9 eV in the N1s (Fig. 2h) and S2p (Fig. 2i) spectra of CDs-ZPEI10k can be deconvoluted into CN+, SC, SO, SO bonds [46], illustrating the existence of zwitterionic groups onto CDs-ZPEI10k. Moreover, the elemental contents (Table. S1) also confirm the successful conversion of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k to CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological behaviors of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k under water lubrication
ce:section.ce:para: It is recognized that organic molecules and nanoparticles synergistically regulate the tribological properties of nano-additives [47]. Fig. S2a shows the evolution of COF curves achieved by water and CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k (1.0 wt%)/water lubricants. The COF curve of water fluctuates and climbs throughout the test period, and the mean COF reaches 0.561, suggesting the weak lubricity of water. Compared with water, all the COF curves of lubricants are stable, smooth, and low, intuitively explaining the favorable friction-reducing function of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k. The average COFs of lubricants reduce to 0.177, 0.224, and 0.260 when the molecular weight of the PEI is 10 k, 1.8 k, and 0.6 k, respectively. As displayed in Fig. S2b, the 61.4 % and 68.4 %, 56.9 % and 60.0 %, and 46.8 % and 53.7 % reductions in mean wear rates and COFs for water can be achieved by blending CDs-ZPEI10k, CDs-ZPEI1.8k, and CDs-ZPEI0.6k, correspondingly, indicating that the lubricity of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k is strongly related to the molecular weight of ZPEI shell. As expected, the CDs-ZPEI10k is regarded as the best additive. The above viewpoints also can be confirmed by the profiles and micrographs of wear tracks provided in Fig. S3.

To disclose the lubrication contribution of CDs core and ZPEI shell, the tribological behaviors of CDs-PEI10k and PEI10k as additives for water lubrication are estimated and compared with CDs-ZPEI10k under the same conditions. The contact angles in Fig. S4 illustrate that the wettability of water on a fresh steel surface can be significantly promoted by adding CDs-ZPEI10k, implying the desired interfacial adhesion behavior of the ZPEI10k shell. As evidenced by Fig. 3 a, the COF curve of PEI10k (1.0 wt%)/water lubricant is slightly lower than that of water, and the lubricant stabilizes the mean COF at a higher value (0.503), indicating the inferior lubricity of PEI10k. Compared with PEI10k, the COF curve of CDs-PEI10k (1.0 wt%)/water lubricant shows drastic fluctuation, and the mean COF reduces to 0.337, disclosing the desired friction-reducing ability of CDs core. As exhibited in Fig. 3b, upon the addition of PEI10k, the average wear and friction reductions for water are merely 16.5 % and 10.4 %, which are less than that of CDs-PEI10k (33.3 % and 39.8 %), directly explaining the tremendous load-carrying capacity of CDs core. Profiting from the strong hydration and adsorption effects given by copious hydrophilic and charged sites of the ZPEI10k shell, the lubricity of CDs-ZPEI10k is clearly superior to CDs-PEI10k. The aforesaid results, coupled with Fig. S5, elucidate that the CDs core and ZPEI0.6-10 k shells of CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k played a synergistic role in enhancing the water lubrication.

The concentration (c) is a crucial parameter governing the tribological properties of additives that should be precisely quantified [48,49]. Fig. 4 a displays the variation of COF curves achieved by the lubricants with different c of CDs-ZPEI10k. When the c of CDs-ZPEI10k is lower than 0.7 wt%, the COF curves of lubricants exhibit a steep upward process and then remain stable until the test is completed. As expected, the COF curves of lubricants gradually become low, stable, and smooth with growing c. The COF curve achieved by CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant is the lowest and smoothest. However, the tribological properties of the lubricant attenuated when the c of CDs-ZPEI10k is higher than 1.5 wt%, reflecting the commonality of abrasive wear triggered by overmuch nano-additives [50,51]. As shown in Fig. S6, numerous scratches can be observed from the rubbed surface lubricated by CDs-ZPEI10k (2.0 wt%)/water lubricant, intuitively reflecting the abrasive wear caused by excessive nanoparticles. Fig. 4b compares the mean wear rates and COFs gained by the lubricants. As evidenced by the 72.5 % and 66.1 % reductions in friction and wear, the optimal c of CDs-ZPEI10k is further deemed as 1.5 wt%. The profiles and micrographs of wear tracks lubricated with water and CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant are presented in Fig. 4c ∼ 4d. The width and depth of the wear track achieved by the lubricant are 275.6 and 2.7 μm, less than 378.9 and 7.5 μm obtained by water. Distinctly, the wear track achieved by water is rougher, deeper, and wider than that of lubricant, directly accounting for the tremendous anti-wear function of CDs-ZPEI10k.

The tribological stability of lubricant under harsh conditions is pivotal to its practical application. The influences of heavy load and high frequency on the tribological performances of water and CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant are investigated. As exhibited in Fig. 5 a ∼ 5b, the COF curves of water at 75 N and 10 Hz fluctuate sharply between 0.38 and 0.50. Under the identical parameters, the COF curves of the lubricant are situated at about 0.12, which is significantly smoother and lower than that of water, proving the credible friction-reducing ability of CDs-ZPEI10k over a wide range of load and frequency. Amazingly, the tribological properties of CDs-ZPEI10k did not attenuate significantly with further increases in load and frequency to 150 N and 20 Hz, which directly reflects the superior ball-bearing function of CDs-ZPEI10k. Fig. S7 indicates that the mean wear rates and COFs of the lubricant are 55.7 % and 72.6 % less than water at 75 N, reflecting the reliable load-bearing capacity of CDs-ZPEI10k. The mean wear and friction reductions of lubricant slightly decline to 74.0 % and 60.1 % at 10 Hz, proving the stable tribological behaviors of CDs-ZPEI10k, as shown in Fig. S8. The above results also can be agreed with the profiles and micrographs of wear tracks given in Fig. S9–S10.

The start-stop tests accompanied by the interchange of water and CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant are designed and executed to study the running-in ability of CDs-ZPEI10k in the start-stop period. As shown in Fig. 5c, using water lubrication as stage I, the COF climbs continuously to about 0.62. When the water is replaced with lubricant and stage II is initiated, the COF rapidly declines to about 0.22 and then remains relatively stable, suggesting the robust adsorption ability and deposition stability of CDs-ZPEI10k on the rubbing steel surface. Interestingly, in stage III, the COF of water lubrication rises rapidly and then fluctuates around 0.50, which benefits from a few CDs-ZPEI10k deposited on the rubbed surface during stage II. The zeta potentials shown in Fig. S11 confirm that CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k with copious sulfonate groups are negatively charged, suggesting that CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k can be rapidly adsorbed and deposited on the positively charged friction surface via electrostatic interaction. As depicted in Fig. 5d, the COF curve of lubricant in stage I displays a short running-in period and then remains at about 0.17. When the lubricant is replaced with water and stage II is initiated, the COF curve gradually ascends to 0.57 and then continuously declines to 0.49. A similar regularity also can be found in the following stages, further reflecting the admirable running-in ability and start-stop stability of CDs-ZPEI10k under water lubrication.

The long-term experiments for water and CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant are executed to estimate the service life of CDs-ZPEI10k. As shown in Fig. 5e, the COF curve of water continuously grows from 0.539 to 1.079 and shows drastic fluctuations throughout the experiment. In sharp contrast, the mean COF of the lubricant merely increases from 0.118 to 0.238 and exhibits slight fluctuations, indicating the eminent friction-reducing function of CDs-ZPEI10k. As disclosed by Fig. S12, the 74.1 % and 75.1 % reductions in mean COF and wear rate for water can be gained by adding CDs-ZPEI10k, reflecting the tribological stability of CDs-ZPEI10k can be well-remained in long-running operation by the synergistic effects of hydration, adsorption, and deposition. The above conclusions also can be drawn from the profiles and micrographs of wear tracks given in Fig. S13.

SEM images are collected to disclose the morphological features of the wear tracks achieved by water and CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, typical pits and cracks corresponding to fatigue damage exist widely on the wear track achieved by water, which reflects the mediocre anti-wear and anti-fatigue behaviors of water. Fig. 6b indicates that the wear track achieved by the lubricant is smooth and narrow, suggesting the superior anti-wear and friction-reducing properties of CDs-ZPEI10k. In addition, many deposits and small scratches can be observed from the wear track achieved by the lubricant, directly explaining the polishing and repairing function of CDs-ZPEI10k. The above results also can be confirmed by EDS results in Fig. S14–S15 and Table S2, where the N and S fractions on the wear track achieved by water are significantly less than that of lubricant, further reflecting the deposition of CDs-ZPEI10k during the friction process.

XPS spectra are given to reveal the chemical and elemental compositions on the wear tracks achieved by water and CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6c, the Fe3p (54.8 eV), Fe3s (93.0 eV), Fe2p (711.4 and 725.4 eV), and Fe auger (784.5 eV) peaks in the full-scan spectra on the wear track achieved by the lubricant are distinctly weaker than that of water [52], hinting at the deposition of CDs-ZPEI10k over the rubbed steel substrate. The Fe2p spectrum in Fig. 6d indicates the primary Fe forms are Fe0 (706.5 eV), Fe2+ (727.9, 718.3, and 710.2 eV), and Fe3+ (732.7, 724.2, and 713.3 eV) [53,54]. Notably, the Fe0 signals in the wear track achieved by water are slightly lower than that of the lubricant. The Fe–C (283.4 eV) and Fe–O (529.5 eV) species can be discerned from the C1s and O1s spectra presented in Fig. 6e ∼ 6f, respectively, indicating the formation of iron carbides and oxides on the wear tracks [55]. It should be noted that the C and N elements on the wear track achieved by water should originate from the steel substrate and atmosphere [56,57]. Compared with the spectra of the wear track achieved by water, the intense CO/CS (288.3 eV), CO/CN+ (286.1 eV), SO (533.4 eV), CO/CO (531.8 eV), and SO (530.7 eV) peaks can be fitted from the C1s and O1s spectra of the wear track achieved by the lubricant [58,59]. As shown in Fig. 6g ∼ 6h, the N and S signals in the N1s and S2p spectra of the wear track achieved by the lubricant are distinctly stronger than those of water, directly disclosing the deposition of CDs-ZPEI10k. The element contents summarized in Table S3 consolidate the above conclusions.

The microstructural and morphological information of the tribofilm on the wear track achieved by CDs-ZPEI10k (1.5 wt%)/water lubricant is disclosed by TEM and EDS results. The TEM image in Fig. 7 a shows that the thickness of the tribofilm between the steel substrate and the Pt-cap layer is about 320 nm. Fig. 7b exhibits that the tribofilm is composed of an amorphous matrix and numerous nanoparticles. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) proves the main species in the tribofilm matrix are FeO (111), Fe2O3 (004), and graphitized carbon (002) [60,61]. As revealed by the HRTEM image in Fig. 7c, the single nanoparticle shows a clear core–shell structure consisting of an amorphous shell and a crystalline core (lattice spacing of 0.24 nm), proving that the CDs-ZPEI10k is compacted into the tribofilm matrix [62,63]. HAADF image in Fig. 7d, EDS elemental maps in Fig. 7e–j, and line-scanning results in Fig. 7k collectively confirm that the tribofilm is rich in Fe, C, O, N, and S, likely a hybrid system of iron compounds and CDs-ZPEI10k. The above results suggest that the tribofilm embedded with CDs-ZPEI10k is crucial in reducing friction, wear, and fatigue damage under water lubrication.

According to the tribological results and worn surface analyses, the water lubrication mechanism of CDs-ZPEI10k is logically deduced and proposed in Fig. 8 . At the running-in stage, the escape of low-energy electrons caused the exposure of Fe3+ and Fe2+ on the rubbing surfaces [64,65]. Benefiting from the intense electronegativity of sulfonate groups, the CDs-ZPEI10k can be rapidly adsorbed and deposited on the positively charged friction surface via electrostatic interaction, mitigating the direct collision of friction pairs. The deposited CDs-ZPEI10k as ball bearings withstood not only the load but also polished and repaired the rubbed surfaces. Due to the intrinsic hydration repulsion and ionic solvation effects of zwitterionic segments, a robust hydration layer can form onto the deposited CDs-ZPEI10k, thus significantly shortening the running-in time and providing stable hydration lubrication. The charged hydration layer further promotes the deposition of CDs-ZPEI10k. Moreover, the abundant organic components on the deposited layer can react with the active metallic surface under friction heat and pressure, inducing the compaction of a uniform CDs-ZPEI10k-based tribofilm. In other words, the adsorption, hydration, and deposition of CDs-ZPEI10k played synergistic roles in improving the tribological behaviors of water.

In this study, a class of polyzwitterionic CDs (CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k) are conceived and synthesized by a simple ring-opening reaction. The physicochemical features of CDs-PEI0.6-10 k and CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k are disclosed by morphological, structural, spectral, and thermal analyses. As the additives for water lubrication, the CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k show excellent dispersion stability and tribological behaviors. The ZPEI0.6-10 k shells with copious hydrophilic and charged groups provide CDs-ZPEI0.6-10 k with strong adsorption and hydration properties on the rubbing steel surfaces, thus significantly improving the hydration lubrication. As the best additive, the CDs-ZPEI10k at the optimal c of 1.5 wt% reduces the wear and friction of water by 66.1 % and 72.5 %, respectively. Moreover, the CDs-ZPEI10k shows robust tribological properties under heavy load (150 N), high frequency (20 Hz), start-stop, and long-term tests. The tribological evaluations and worn surface characterizations confirm that the adsorption, hydration, and deposition effects synergistically expedite the generation of the CDs-ZPEI10k-based tribofilm with superior friction and fatigue control behaviors. This study proposes a feasible strategy for designing and synthesizing polyzwitterionic CDs, enabling a high-efficiency aqueous lubrication system.
